food_subgroup: Red lentils (Lens culinaris), split, dried raw

ontology.foodex2_description: Lentils, split red/orange (masoor dal), raw dried

main_ingredients: red lentils (Lens culinaris subsp. culinaris), split, dehusked

serving.natural_language_portion: 50g dry masoor dal (approximately ¼ cup) yields approximately 100–120g cooked lentils — a standard side dish portion or component of a dal curry serving.

nutrient_source.title: Red Lentils / Masoor Dal — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Masoor Dal (dried, split). Cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy lentils raw (FDC ID 172420). Values per 100g dry raw lentils.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled red lentils (approximately 26). Dry lentil GI is not applicable. Red lentils have one of the lowest GI values of all foods due to their high protein and fibre content creating a viscous gel that dramatically slows carbohydrate digestion.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high in potassium (677mg/100g dry) and magnesium (47mg/100g) — both clinically associated with blood pressure reduction. Extremely low in sodium (6mg/100g). Rich in folate which supports vascular endothelial function.
hypertension.guidance: Masoor dal is one of the most cardiovascular-friendly foods available. Daily inclusion of lentil-based dishes (dal) is recommended for patients with hypertension. The potassium-to-sodium ratio is exceptionally favourable. Cook without adding excessive salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Red lentils have an exceptionally low GI (~26) when boiled. Very high in dietary fibre (10.7g/100g dry) and protein (25.1g). One of the most evidence-backed foods for postprandial blood glucose control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Masoor dal is strongly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes as a daily protein and fibre source. Replacing part of the rice or roti portion with dal reduces the overall meal glycaemic load substantially. Paired with vegetables, dal provides a complete meal with minimal glycaemic impact.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extraordinarily high in folate (479mcg DFE/100g dry) — essential for one-carbon metabolism and immune regulation. Substantial iron (7.6mg) and zinc (3.3mg). Lentils provide plant-based anti-inflammatory compounds.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red lentils are highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a daily protein source. Folate supplementation is often recommended alongside methotrexate treatment for RA — dietary folate from lentils and other pulses complements this. Anti-inflammatory spicing with turmeric and ginger further enhances the therapeutic value of dal dishes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red lentils (masoor dal) are small, orange-red, split and dehusked lentils (Lens culinaris). They cook quickly without soaking — unlike whole pulses — because their outer hull has been removed. When boiled, they collapse and form a thick, creamy orange porridge-like texture ideal for dal curries and soups. Among the most protein-dense plant foods (25.1g/100g dry) with an exceptionally low GI (~26). Extraordinarily high in folate (479mcg DFE/100g) — making them one of the richest folate sources in the food supply. Used throughout India, Bangladesh, the Middle East, and increasingly worldwide. Common in Singapore Indian and Pakistani restaurants as dal soup or masoor dal curry.
common_user_queries: how long to cook masoor dal
is masoor dal good for weight loss

confidence_reason: Exact match in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Masoor Dal (dried), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy.
notes: Values per 100g dry raw split masoor dal. Cook quickly without soaking (15–20 minutes boiling). The iron in lentils is non-haem iron with lower bioavailability than haem iron — consuming with vitamin C-rich foods (tomato, lime) enhances absorption. Boiling reduces anti-nutritional factors (phytates, trypsin inhibitors) and increases iron/zinc bioavailability.
